mainmatter.p: Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is an embedding.
We must show that each fiber of f is a proposition; in fact, each fiber is contractible as biinvertible maps are contractible, and contractible types are propositions.

Let f \colon A \to B have a quasi-inverse g \colon B \to A with incoherent counit \epsilon \colon f \circ g \sim 1_{ B } and unit \eta \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A }. We can correct the counit to obtain a coherent inverse to f \colon A \to B.
The corrected counit \epsilon ' \colon g \circ f \sim 1_{ A } is defined like so:
mainmatter.tex: \epsilon ' x : \equiv \epsilon \mathopen {} \left ( f \mathopen {} \left ( f ^{-1} x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} ^{-1} \cdot \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \mathopen {} \left ( \eta \mathopen {} \left ( f ^{-1} x \right ) \mathclose {} \right ) \mathclose {} \cdot \epsilon x
mainmatter.p: The coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon ' \circ f can be constructed using the naturality of homotopies and the coherence of homotopies onto the identity.

Let f \colon A \to B be biinvertible with s,r \colon B \to A and h_s \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } and h_r \colon r \circ f = 1_{ A }. Then we may define a homotopy h' \colon s \circ f \sim 1_{ A } exhibiting s \colon B \to A as a retraction of f \colon A \to B. Therefore, we may define a quasi-inverse to f tracked by s \colon B \to A.
We have s \circ f \sim r \circ f \circ s \circ f by h_r and r \circ f \circ s \circ f \sim r \circ f by h_s and r \circ f \sim 1_{ A } by h_r.
Any invertible map f \colon A \to B is contractible.

Let \epsilon \colon f \circ f ^{-1} = 1_{ B } be the counit of the inverse to f \colon A \to B. Given b:B, we must show that the fiber of f over b is contractible; the centre of contraction is given by the pair \mathopen {} \left ( f ^{-1} x, \epsilon x \right ) \mathclose {}, and the required paths can be constructed via the univalence principle for fibers using the unit \eta \colon f ^{-1} \circ f = 1_{ A }, the coherence \chi \colon \mathsf {ap}_{ f } \circ \eta \sim \epsilon \circ f, and the naturality of homotopies.
Any contractible map f \colon A \to B is a section.

Let f \colon A \to B be contractible; we define a retraction r \colon B \to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. To construct h_r \colon r \circ f \sim 1_{ A } exhibiting r as a retraction of f, we use the identification between any two elements of the fiber of f over fx.
Any contractible map f \colon A \to B is a retraction.

Let f \colon A \to B be contractible; we define a section s \colon B \to A to take b:B to the first component of the centre of contraction of the fiber of f over b. The witness h_s \colon f \circ s \sim 1_{ B } exhibiting s as a section of f, we use the second component of the centre of contraction.
Any contractible map f \colon A \to B is a biinvertible.
Because contractible maps are both sections and retractions.
Any quasi-invertible map f \colon A \to B is contractible.
Any quasi-inverse can be improved to a coherent inverse, and any coherently invertible map is contractible.
Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is contractible.
As biinvertible maps are quasi-invertible and quasi-invertible maps are contractible.

Let f \colon A \to B be biinvertible; then for any type C, the postcomposition map \mathopen {} \left ( f \circ - \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {} \left ( C \to A \right ) \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left ( C \to B \right ) \mathclose {} is biinvertible.

The section and retraction are built using by postcomposing with the designated section and retraction of f; the witnesses for the postcomposition sections and retractions are constructed using the univalence principle for function spaces.

Let f \colon A \to B be biinvertible; then for any type C, the precomposition map \mathopen {} \left ( - \circ f \right ) \mathclose {} \colon \mathopen {} \left ( B \to C \right ) \mathclose {} \to \mathopen {} \left ( A \to C \right ) \mathclose {} is biinvertible.
Analogous to the corresponding result for pushforward along biinvertible maps.
Let f \colon A \to B be a section of some r \colon B \to A; the type of sections of f is, then, a proposition.

Using the univalence principle for dependent function spaces, we can see that the type of sections of f \colon A \to B is a retract of the fiber of f \circ - at 1_{ B }, using the univalence principle for sections.

To show that the type of sections of f is a proposition, we may assume beforehand that we have such a section of f; therefore, because we have assumed a retraction of f already, we know that f is biinvertible and therefore that f is contractible. Because propositions are closed under retracts, it suffices to show that the fiber of f \circ - at 1_{ B } is a proposition, which follows because f is contractible.
Let f \colon A \to B be a retraction of some s \colon B \to A; the type of retractions of f is, then, a proposition.
Analogous to the corresponding result for sections of sections, using the fact that the type of retractions of f can be seen to be a retract of the fiber of - \circ f at 1_{ A }.
The type of biinverses of a given function f \colon A \to B is a proposition.

tree.mainmatter: Sections of sections and retractions of retractions are unique.

mainmatter.p: Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is an embedding.
We must show that each fiber of f is a proposition; in fact, each fiber is contractible as biinvertible maps are contractible, and contractible types are propositions.
Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is surjective.
This is immediate, using the unit map of propositional truncation and the section of f.
Any bijection f \colon A \to B is contractible.

Fixing b:B, we must check that the fiber of f over b is contractible. For the centre of contraction, we must construct an element of this fiber; because f is an embedding, the fiber is a proposition, so it suffices by the induction principle for propositional truncation to observe that the fiber is inhabited because f is surjective.
Any bijection f \colon A \to B is biinvertible.
By , bijections are contractible; by , bijections are contractible.
Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is a bijection.
By and .
The correspondence between biinvertible and bijective maps of and lifts to an equivalence from the type of equivalences to the type of bijections.
By and via .
Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is contractible.
As biinvertible maps are quasi-invertible and quasi-invertible maps are contractible.
If a type A is contractible, then it is a proposition.
As A is contractible with some centre a:A, we have for any x,y:A identifications \chi _x \colon a = x and \chi _y \colon a = y. Therefore, we identify x with y using \chi _x ^{-1} \cdot \chi _y.

A function f \colon A \to B is called n-truncated when all of its fibers are n-truncated types. By analogy, we shall also refer to maps as contractible when their fibers are; we shall refer to maps as embeddings when their fibers are propositions.

A type A is said to be contractible with centre a:A when every x:A is equipped with an identification \chi _x \colon a = x. We say that A is contractible when it comes equipped with some a:A such that A is contractible with centre a. We sometimes refer to a contractible type as a singleton.
The fiber of a function f \colon A \to B at an element b:B is the type consisting of elements a \colon A equipped with identifications p \colon fa=b.
A type A is said to be a proposition when every x:A, the type A is contractible with centre x, i.e. all elements of A are identified.
A biinverse to a function f \colon A \to B is defined to be a pair of a section and a retraction to f.
Any biinvertible map f \colon A \to B is a bijection.
By and .
